words: SAM SCHRAGER: I want to know how the Steffen family came to locate out here.
KENNETH STEFFEN: Well, this grandfather, he supposed to divorce his first wife,
my father,mother in the east, you know, and he took my father and his sister
Mary with him.Then the rest stayed with the mother, see. So anyway, he
married again. And he married a woman name of Floser. And had this family.
And his brother-in-law was named and he was a German-Canadian.
And he settled and homesteaded over in Union Flat here. Ply grandfather
settled up at Farmington. Now I heard that a long time afterward. As a youngster
I didn't know that at all, but I used to go over and visit this
over in Pullman. 'Cause my father and him was good friends all the time
see.'Cause he was a youngster, he probably knew him, stayed around. Well
it seemed like my father was 39 when he married, but during that time he
left, he wouldn't stay with his father very long. And the girl Mary, she
married somebody name of, lived over in Shelton, Washington, around
in there. And then she had a daughter and that daughter and granddaughter
visit me here one time and I had an address someplace, they lived in Oregon.
But I only roe 'em one time. But my sister kept correspondence with them.

speaker: SS
words: How did your grand move to Moscow?

speaker: KS
words: Well,they got acquainted this way:Now my father tells me this and I'm quite

speaker: KS
words: young. He said he was a teamster working for the army on the Mexican border.

speaker: KS
words: Dohn Charles Fremont Steff«n, named after 3ohn Charles Fremont the explorer

speaker: KS
words: you know, and army general that did a lot of surveying in the west. Someone

speaker: KS
words: said there was a man here in the army name of Steffen. Any relation. Said

speaker: KS
words: I don't know. He didn't know he had a brother or sister, he was too

speaker: KS
words: young. They met and they got to talking and it was Franklin and he was

speaker: KS
words: the oldest one of the boys and he was in the army. He eventually I think

speaker: KS
words: retired.. That's how my father met. Well he'd been out east and west, back

speaker: KS
words: and forth, see, and as near as I know, that's the reason they come, but

speaker: KS
words: I don't know for sure at all. I know that the other family come out. So

speaker: KS
words: anyway, and they came out here, why, Will had this little piece of property

speaker: KS
words: and how he worked and so on, I don't know, but he brought his mother,'cause

speaker: KS
words: his family stayed together that way. Henry, he homesteaded down at Agatha

speaker: KS
words: on this side of the Clearwater River was Agatha, was just a little railroad

speaker: KS
words: station right across the river. And after Will was killed, or killed himself

speaker: KS
words: here, he didn't get, they didn't hirii, they can talk all they want to.

speaker: KS
words: My grandmother, according to my mother, had the pistol under her skirt when

speaker: KS
words: she waved the white flag at 'em to quit shooting at the house.Hearsay stuff.

speaker: SS
words: That's all we got.

speaker: KS
words: So anyway, Henry took my grandmother to live with him down here on that

speaker: KS
words: homestead at Agatha. Now in there, there"s this girl, Louella, she's a sister.

speaker: KS
words: And my father met her later on and knew her, she's only four or five years

speaker: KS
words: old yet. She married a man named Ferdinand and he was from Germany.

speaker: KS
words: And he was a Spanish-American war veteran. He went to the Philipines to

speaker: KS
words: fight there, see. Come back. Well then they homesteaded down there right

speaker: KS
words: next to this Uncle Henry see. I can take you right by the place. The old

speaker: KS
words: barnhouse still stand. I been down there as a youngster, by God, four five,

speaker: KS
words: six, seven, eight years old.

speaker: SS
words: That's near Duliaetta, right?

speaker: KS
words: Well you go out Guliaetta, there's a road crosses that little Potlatch creek

speaker: KS
words: and go.over the hill. The Clearwater, see, and you go down there, like about

speaker: KS
words: a mile or two miles being down to the Clearwater River. They get all their

speaker: KS
words: wood off the Clearwater River in the spring by roping it and pulling it

speaker: KS
words: in, logs and everything floating down the river, years ago. But when Henry

speaker: KS
words: had my grandmother down there, he and some neighbor got into a scuffle about

speaker: KS
words: some hog running lose, what it was I don't know. But anyway, get in the

speaker: KS
words: garden, or some damn thing. He was setting at the table eating his dinner

speaker: KS
words: at night, it was dark, the light was on, grandma was serving him, this guy

speaker: KS
words: shot through the window and killed him. So she had two sons killed.

speaker: SS
words: Your grandmother saw him get killed?

speaker: KS
words: She was right there, see. So then this aunt had her come and live

speaker: KS
words: with her.

speaker: SS
words: What happened to this fella that shot him?

speaker: KS
words: Well, he got ten years.(laughs) That's all he got out of it, and I don't

speaker: KS
words: know how many years he served. I don't know what the name was, but I was

speaker: KS
words: very, very young, seven or eight years old, something like that, when got

speaker: KS
words: word in Moscow that my father went down, went to the trial, it was down

speaker: KS
words: in Lewiston too, see.Then Mrs. took care of this grandmother

speaker: KS
words: until she passed away. Now you must remember she's getting up in years quite

speaker: KS
words: a bit. Didn't speak very much English and my mother couldn't understand her

speaker: KS
words: you know, very well, so they didn't get along too well as I understand,

speaker: KS
words: but I think my mother should have made more of an effort too, but she was

speaker: KS
words: Prussian-German, quite set in her ways, see.And you got books wrote that

speaker: KS
words: Will was mean to his mother, that was a crock of shit, by gosh. He was good

speaker: KS
words: to her, took care of her. And so did Henry took care of her. And then Louella

speaker: KS
words: took care of her..He came into Moscow, and hell, I was so little I can just

speaker: KS
words: merely remember him. He helped lay the sewers and the water mains in Moscow

speaker: KS
words: used to have privies and wells, you know. He worked on that and he ended

speaker: KS
words: up living over til he died over here at Richland, Washington. My father

speaker: KS
words: went and brought him over and buried him too, so all of em are buried in

speaker: KS
words: Pullman cemetery. The grandmother and the four boys are. From my father's

speaker: KS
words: first family, see?

speaker: SS
words: Sounds like your grandmother saw a lot. She was born in the old country?

speaker: KS
words: She was born in Germany too, so was my grandfather. Born in Germany.

speaker: SS
words: To see two sons die.

speaker: KS
words: But you know, back in those days,that was something, hell that was the only

speaker: KS
words: one. There's kind of a history talking about it,'cause Dr. Watkins was kind

speaker: KS
words: of popular. But hell,there was all kinds of shooting around, killing. There's

speaker: KS
words: a guy shot his neighbor over a fence out in Viola. Had 160 acres filrland

speaker: KS
words: out there, Oversmith got it for defending him and got him off! Back there

speaker: KS
words: before WWI, there was lots of things going on around.

speaker: SS
words: You told me there was one that had to do with a drugstore?

speaker: KS
words: I think his name was Willis. By god, his wife, he got pretty dcunk, she

speaker: KS
words: took a few shots at him, he run down the streets a few times.(laughs)That's

speaker: KS
words: what my father told me. Now I didn't see these things. I'm telling you...

speaker: SS
words: That's all you're saying.

speaker: KS
words: I'm just saying what it is, see.

speaker: SS
words: What did Will do for a living?

speaker: KS
words: He was a butcher here. He had a slaughter house and he butchered cattle

speaker: KS
words: and sold, the whole damn trouble started about. The building's still standing

speaker: KS
words: out there on that place where its, the walls are about two feet thick and

speaker: KS
words: all sawdust^and the door's a foot and a half wide, goes in there you know

speaker: KS
words: and stays tight. Overhead of it the sawdust. At the back one time had a

speaker: KS
words: platform to pull in ice, we pulled in ice and put it in the top over there,

speaker: KS
words: and that stayed all year round in that sawdust. That was a cold room. Trouble

speaker: KS
words: was, Watkins was kind of a crusader. We all needed 'em, I guess. But critizing

speaker: KS
words: or doing something, my Uncle Will's beef. Hell, he took care as good as

speaker: KS
words: anybody else did. In fact, he had the best place there was,he had a better

speaker: KS
words: placettve ever tnougnt of having. 'Cause they had a butcher

speaker: KS
words: and bring right to town, to the cold room in town. The last one of those

speaker: KS
words: kinds of rooms they got out was the Sanitary Meat Market, is up there on

speaker: KS
words: Third street, three buildings in there, you know, before you hit the Market

speaker: KS
words: Time Drug,that was the Sanitary Meat Market, and they had a cold room, same

speaker: KS
words: damn thing, with sawdust walls, by god. Eventually it got fixed a little

speaker: KS
words: better, but before they tore it out, by god that was their cold room.

speaker: SS
words: So your uncle had the meat hanging and he could just leave it there.

speaker: KS
words: It was cold enough, meat should age jwst a little bit. General rule, all

speaker: KS
words: these shopmen, meat, hang it up for two weeks to age. Its much better.

speaker: SS
words: So you think that Watkins didn't think he was keeping his meat good?

speaker: KS
words: Well, I don't think he was mad, I think what he did was brought up that

speaker: KS
words: the meat, something was wrong with it.Now that's what dad said. Course,

speaker: KS
words: that's heresay, 'cause he was killed before dad come back out here. That

speaker: KS
words: was heresay so I don't know anything true about it or not.

speaker: SS
words: But your dad thought that was the reason your uncle went after him.

speaker: KS
words: I tell you, as a young man I grew up, well a man died here, his name was

speaker: KS
words: Tom. Kimbrting. His wife Hattie still living, she had a place over on Elm

speaker: KS
words: Street there, and Hattie, Tom told me he knew Will, hell, he said he was

speaker: KS
words: a hell of a good fella, said he had a temper all right.Carl Smith was our

speaker: KS
words: first fire chief in Moscow,he had a transfer, grey hair you know, had horses

speaker: KS
words: first, trucks, hati- all kinds of moving things, you know. work...

speaker: SS
words: He was the fire chief here from many years.

speaker: KS
words: Yes. Well he knew Will.Those are the only two people I knew that knew Will

speaker: KS
words: that I ever met.

speaker: SS
words: What did he think?

speaker: KS
words: Well, we never talked about it too much. Then our Earl Cornwall, he's older

speaker: KS
words: than I am, he has a paper that you have, a Spokane paper, not the one you

speaker: KS
words: have. But that's taken off that's one that old Earl Cornwall give that to

speaker: KS
words: the photo guy for him to copy that and you got it in that paper, see. I

speaker: KS
words: had both copies here, my mother, see.She had to destroy them.It's too bad,

speaker: KS
words: she's just set in her ways, she doesnfi want anything done about it. But

speaker: KS
words: it's too bad she had to do it. But I had 'em and I want to keep 'em.

speaker: SS
words: What, the way you've heard it, what happened the day they both got killed?

speaker: KS
words: The way it happened: he had his horse, you know. And the watering trough

speaker: KS
words: was on Second Street there. Well anyway, where he met the Doctor at, I don't

speaker: KS
words: know. He shot him and he slumped over his dashboard and his horse trotted

speaker: KS
words: on down to the office is what they said. Anyway, he watered his horse there,

speaker: KS
words: someone said the doctor was killed and Will said,"I did it." And as he rode

speaker: KS
words: off down through Main Street he shot Creighton through the arm. And then

speaker: KS
words: his horse was shot out from under him on the way home. And this here girl

speaker: KS
words: out here, Driscoll, said he went up over Sixth Street hill, why'd he run

speaker: KS
words: a horse^over Sixth Street hill when I think there's an easier route through

speaker: KS
words: there, which he did do, but he lost his horse.The field out there's all

speaker: KS
words: full of shocks, they had gr .ain out there, you know they bound it and shocked

speaker: KS
words: it.It was standing , hid behind that creek too you know. There was three

speaker: KS
words: bridges on a half a mile there one time. They only got the cement one there

speaker: KS
words: now, but there's two on past that.AClear out in the field and come around.

speaker: KS
words: Then they dug it right down along side the road see. Our place there had

speaker: KS
words: the creek run through it. Dad had three acres in there that was uncle Will's.

speaker: KS
words: And we came here when he came here, why he just paid the taxes on it for

speaker: KS
words: several years on it and got was called a tax deed, see. But he did go to

speaker: KS
words: the expense of getting this tombstone for uncle Will, plus he buried my

speaker: KS
words: grandmother and my uncle's over there too.

speaker: SS
words: So Will went home, and then what happened?

speaker: KS
words: Will got home and then he didn't have very much ammunition, they were shooting

speaker: KS
words: at the house all the time. Shooting at it, you know,shot out the windows

speaker: KS
words: I guess. I saw a bullet hole in the house as a youngster, but the house

speaker: KS
words: is burnt down, but he'd run out of bullets so he shot himself. He knew

speaker: KS
words: they was gonna get him, I guess. That's as near as I can find out about

speaker: KS
words: itJ don't know for sure. It's all heresay is all it is.

speaker: SS
words: The story I heard was that all these guys went and broke open the hardware

speaker: SS
words: store and took guns out of there.

speaker: KS
words: Collins had a hardware store, and I don't know, I think everybody had a

speaker: KS
words: gun. Creighton got shot in the arm, by god. I guess he talked when he should

speaker: KS
words: have been listening.(laughs)

speaker: SS
words: Was there notes that they found on him? They said he had a list of men that

speaker: SS
words: he wanted to get.

speaker: KS
words: I heard that too. But my father said he didn't know about it, but I've heard

speaker: KS
words: that thing that he had a list. But you know, after I was a youngster,now

speaker: KS
words: there was had slaughterhouses just

speaker: KS
words: about a quarter mile due east of our house where we lived -there.

speaker: KS
words: And I don't know. Anyway, my father knew how to butcher. All the people

speaker: KS
words: in those days knew how to do it. He used to go up and help him butchac a

speaker: KS
words: lot for these people. But, hogs as well as knofcked beef down.

speaker: SS
words: He worked at ., and Kittley?

speaker: KS
words: Worked steady, but every once in awhile when he wanted to work, but at that

speaker: KS
words: time you know,you didn't save all the things you do today.They save everything

speaker: KS
words: on a pig but the squeal you know. The only thing you throw away on a cow

speaker: KS
words: is the tail.That's only the hard part. But I ate a lot of heart and liver

speaker: KS
words: in those days 'cause it was free, see. We didn't have a place big enough

speaker: KS
words: there. We had a cow and later on oe had a pig or two we'd feed and go out

speaker: KS
words: and butcher, but my mother, the head of the hogs, you know, they'd threw

speaker: KS
words: 'em away. But those days, you know, the wash, a woman had to call a boiler.

speaker: KS
words: Put it on the stove and boil the water, they put soap in and boiled the

speaker: KS
words: dirt out! Anyway, she put couple of these hog heads in, boil 'em for a day.

speaker: KS
words: And clean em up and my father help her and grandmother made head cheese.

speaker: KS
words: Now that's real head cheese, I'll tell you, not like that crap you buy in

speaker: KS
words: the store now. We ate a lot of head cheese in those days. Now they had a

speaker: KS
words: pan that was probably 10 or 12 inches in diameter, 2 inches high. That put

speaker: KS
words: in there, that was settled real good and they cut it out and there was . 5

speaker: KS
words: chunks like that to eat, see. We had a pantry in the house, but we had a

speaker: KS
words: place that my uncle kept cool and we put ice in it, we put things in there

speaker: KS
words: and it would keep fruit, would keep vegetables, we keep everything in there

speaker: KS
words: all winter long, never would freeze and keep nice, see.

speaker: SS
words: Did your father and uncle learn how to butcher before they came out here?

speaker: SS
words: Were they trained?

speaker: KS
words: No, I don't think they were. But the whole thing of it was that everybody

speaker: KS
words: in those days, eell all the farmers, they get together and there would be

speaker: KS
words: what you call a butchering bee, butcher a bunch of hogs. They had an iron

speaker: KS
words: tub and had a fire on the end, the water boiling and dip the old hog in

speaker: KS
words: there and pour out on boards and scrape the hair off of it. But they had

speaker: KS
words: a slide and so on and everybody got doing things like that. And yeujhung

speaker: KS
words: em up and cut 'em open and got doing things like that and those kind, you

speaker: KS
words: either down and salt pork or if you had a smokehouse, you'd use that

speaker: KS
words: there, put all different things on it, salt 'em to beat hell, you know and

speaker: KS
words: smoke 'em, see. You didn't have this type of way of taking care of your

speaker: KS
words: meat like you got today. Some of 'em used to can some meat too, you know,

speaker: KS
words: that was good, have something real quick. But mostlyyall the hog meat I

speaker: KS
words: ever had when I was a kid was salted, went down in a big wooden barrel,

speaker: KS
words: salted, see. We made a barrel of saurkraut every fall too. That was just

speaker: KS
words: kraut and put your salt on it, it goes down and ferments, had a board over

speaker: KS
words: the top of it and a rock to hold it down in there. That saurkraut was the

speaker: KS
words: best medicine a kid ever took.

speaker: SS
words: Did it help your ills?

speaker: KS
words: I tell you, you drink a glass of sauEekraut, in 15 minutes, you take a crap,

speaker: KS
words: it just runs right through you praciiclly.

speaker: SS
words: Cleans you out?

speaker: KS
words: I'll say it'll clean you out. Saurkraut, used to be buy it in

speaker: KS
words: the stores in a can, you know, saurkraut juice, but now I don't think they

speaker: KS
words: put it out anymore, but it was good. We used to drive clear over to the

speaker: KS
words: Germans over there at Uniontown and bring back two or three sacks of cabbage,

speaker: KS
words: by god, and then had a board and cut it, you know, slice it off and work

speaker: KS
words: it so you wouldn't cut your hand and $*t it in there and push it back and

speaker: KS
words: forth over these cutters, you know. That way it would go down in the barrel

speaker: KS
words: and so on. You had to do things like that to have anything to eat.

speaker: SS
words: Your family did it because it was old German custom?

speaker: KS
words: Oh yes, but then a lot of people had it too. It was a damn good thing to

speaker: KS
words: have, saurkraut.

speaker: SS
words: What other parts of the pig did you use? You must have used most of it.

speaker: KS
words: You take the jowls of a hog head, you know, you cut that off, you have

speaker: KS
words: jowl bacon, they call it.

speaker: SS
words: They call it bacon?

speaker: KS
words: You could cure it. They take the whole thing and boil it off good and cut

speaker: KS
words: it out and everything you know goes into making this headcheese. I don't

speaker: KS
words: have the recipe, I wouldn't have no idea. I was young in those days. The

speaker: KS
words: last memory I have of doing it out there I probably was 12 years old. That's

speaker: KS
words: good eating* Course, we raised quite a garden. We used the various things

speaker: KS
words: in the ground. Did you ever see how they pit vegetables and potatoes? You

speaker: KS
words: dig a hole in the ground maybe about a foot and a half, two foot deep and

speaker: KS
words: you put a lot of straw in it and you put some burlap in it or any oidcloth

speaker: KS
words: and put your potatoes in there and different vegetables and you cover it

speaker: KS
words: over with a cloth and you cover it over with lots of straw and then you

speaker: KS
words: put the dirt over it about a foot and a half thick..It can't freeze, see.

speaker: KS
words: When you wanted anything,you open a little hole in it and got in there,

speaker: KS
words: my dad pushed me in there to pull out stuff. He didn't want it too bit} so

speaker: KS
words: anytime, pull out them potatoes, by gosh, and carrots and like that and

speaker: KS
words: then he'd close it up good, put the dirt back on.If it was too frozen,why

speaker: KS
words: go get a wheelbarrow full of manure. See, that would stop it up so no air

speaker: KS
words: could get in and freeze it.

speaker: SS
words: Did that work better than a root cellar?

speaker: KS
words: Worked about the same thing, see, but that way you didn't have any expenses,

speaker: KS
words: by god, only just your labor, see. You can do it pretty easy. A root cellar

speaker: KS
words: it's just like a cellar inside of a hill, boarded up and everything else,

speaker: KS
words: it keeps things from freezing see. Ordinarily, its like that building out

speaker: KS
words: there we had. They didn't freeze in that building either. Mother had her

speaker: KS
words: fruit in it and so on like that. But you didn't have too much room so the

speaker: KS
words: potatoes were buried in the pit lots of times.

speaker: SS
words: Did you get a whole bunch dug at one tiae so you wouldn't have to go back

speaker: SS
words: very often?

speaker: KS
words: Oh yeah, they'd get out enough to last for two or three weeks probably.

speaker: KS
words: They got quite a little bit. But hell, you probably buried aboyt 12 or lb;

speaker: KS
words: sacks of potatoes in there.

speaker: SS
words: Did you garden much in those days?

speaker: KS
words: You had to raise everything. You didn't go to town and buy anything in a

speaker: KS
words: grocery store. You didn't have anything like that. You made butter, took

speaker: KS
words: it, put it, you had your own thing to put in one pound or teo pound thing

speaker: KS
words: and you had your own wrapper on it. And you brought it in the grocery store

speaker: KS
words: and they put it in there and people come in and„ buy, knew what day you

speaker: KS
words: were gonna bring it, they'd buy butter, see. They didn't even have any

speaker: KS
words: refrigeration for it! But you had it wrapped up good in kind of a silken

speaker: KS
words: like, paper like, you know. But you could buy some lemons and some oranges,

speaker: KS
words: you know. And you could buy potatoes and a few things like that in a

speaker: KS
words: grocery store. But, sugar, you know, all those kinds of things. But you

speaker: KS
words: didn't have these type of vegetables in the store in those days you

speaker: KS
words: now. You didn't have, in season, yeah, you might find some in season.

speaker: SS
words: Did your father make a living doing this stuff? Or did he have to work out

speaker: SS
words: too?

speaker: KS
words: He was a teamster but he was of that cemetery in Moscow for two

speaker: KS
words: or three years one time. Got 60 dollars a month. Got paid in three twenty

speaker: KS
words: dollar gold pieces. I remember seeing 'em. We had that little acreage, didn't

speaker: KS
words: pay nothing, only taxes was only ten or twelve dollars a year. We raised

speaker: KS
words: potatoes, raised garden, lot of garden, had few fruit trees on it. Had

speaker: KS
words: the team, did work with a team. Had enough, had some hay doon there, had

speaker: KS
words: some hay for 'em. He raised a colt every year, maybe two colts, sold 'em,

speaker: KS
words: and those things happened you know. They don't take so much to make a living

speaker: KS
words: back then. He didn't want very much, I remember the first telephone we got.

speaker: KS
words: Cost a dollar a month for it put in. I can remember the first phonograph

speaker: KS
words: we had, we had an old Edison, circular disc, by god. But we didn't have

speaker: KS
words: sense enough to keep it, it'd be worth something today.

speaker: SS
words: What about the first car?

speaker: KS
words: Well, we didn't have cars in those days. We had horses. I had pony up til

speaker: KS
words: I mas In my 20's. I didn't have a car. I had some damn good ponies to ride

speaker: KS
words: But then, I had a car before I went in the service too. The first car I

speaker: KS
words: had was a Ford T model.

speaker: SS
words: You used to get where you want to go on ponies?

speaker: KS
words: Well we rode the trains, go to Spokane, go to Colfax or Pullman you rode

speaker: KS
words: the train all the time. Didn't cost only twenty five cents, thirty cents

speaker: KS
words: to ride over to Pullman in the train. We had a little bug, they called it,

speaker: KS
words: one car. from here to Colfax and back. Went down twice a day.

speaker: SS
words: Service was good?

speaker: KS
words: Oh yes. That was the Great Northern, but there was electric train from Moscow

speaker: KS
words: to Spokane. The depot still stands there/dpwn on A Street. That was all

speaker: KS
words: electric come through there. All the freight come in,all the cars weren't

speaker: KS
